turnkey-trade-show-services.title: TURNKEY TRADE SHOW SERVICES
turnkey-trade-show-services.heroLead: Turnkey trade show services in Lexington means one shop owns design through strike, so the exhibitor signs one contract instead of stitching five vendors together for every show.
intro: Turnkey work suits exhibitors who want a single point of accountability for the booth, the freight, the show-services paperwork, and the on-floor crew.
The trade-off is real: less internal coordination from the client side, but a shop that has to actually own every handoff between drawing and dock door.
In Lexington, that usually means one project lead handles the Central Bank Center exhibitor manual, the labor calls, the graphics print, and the AV order on the same calendar.
The pages below cover what falls inside a turnkey scope, what stays outside it, and how a Lexington program tends to run when the same team owns the whole arc.
focusList: Keep TURNKEY TRADE SHOW SERVICES tied to the actual show outcome, not a vague category label.
Treat the venue manual, freight timing, and access plan as the baseline for the build.
Align graphics, AV, and utility requests with the same calendar as the booth work.
Leave room for storage, reuse, and return freight so the next cycle starts cleanly.
Give the floor one clear lead so live decisions stay controlled during install and strike.

With venue side closed, production turns on. The shop builds, the print runs, the AV gear pre-progs, the install plan drafts.
The install runs under one named lead. No rotating crew, no remote account manager: one person on the floor with full context.
Through show hours the lead stays. Anything that comes up at the booth (tech, sponsor, venue) handles without the client team leaving visitors.
Strike honors the venue's posted move-out, then a same-day debrief documents what held and what changes for the next show.

sections.heading: Pricing and scheduling
paragraphs: Pricing for trade show AV production scales with the venue's published rates, the booth's footprint, the freight pattern, and the labor calls. There's no single number that fits every program.

The variables that matter: structure (custom, modular, or rental), graphics, venue service fees, freight, supervision days, and any rigging or AV that pushes outside standard line items. Two trade show AV production programs with different footprints are different projects.
AV production runs against the show's run-of-show document, with content lockup typically 1 to 2 weeks before doors and gear pre-prog and load-in 1 to 3 days before show open.

sections.heading: What a successful project looks like
paragraphs: The shape of a good trade show AV production project: install on schedule, venue walk-through passes, show hours run without callouts, strike honors the posted window.
The numbers that matter on trade show AV production are leads, meetings, and pipeline. The booth and the install are inputs to those metrics.
Post-show, the floor data tells a better story than any pre-show model. The debrief documents what worked and what didn't.

sections.heading: What makes our work different
paragraphs: Trade Show Services of Lexington runs trade show AV production as a local team. Design, fabrication, graphics, and show-site crews share the same shop and the same playbook, which is how quality survives across a multi-show calendar.
Working with a Lexington-based team matters most when something breaks on install day. Solutions live within driving distance instead of a coast away, which compresses the response window.

The compounding effect across a year is real. Trade show av production that runs the second time is faster than the first; the third runs on memory and documentation rather than first-principles planning.

intro: Freight planning starts with the show's published move-in schedule and the venue's drayage program. Central Bank Center routes shipments through its own dock; hotel events at the Hyatt, Hilton, or Marriott City Center work through their loading dock instead.
A clean freight plan covers outbound from the shop, advanced warehouse versus direct-to-show decision, drayage costs, return routing, and the storage destination after strike.
Manifests, weight tickets, and crate labels get written before anything rolls. A truck that shows up without proper paperwork gets sent to the back of the line.
These pages cover the freight scope, the drayage cost line, and how the return cycle plugs into the next show on the calendar.

sections.heading: Lexington trade show market
paragraphs: The Lexington market for expo logistics and shipping runs on a small set of venues and a clear regional drive radius. Central Bank Center anchors downtown; the host-hotel ring carries the breakouts; the Bluegrass corridor extends the audience.

The city's identity (university, healthcare, equine, bourbon, manufacturing) gives programs more than one buyer story to work with, which is why Lexington pulls real recurring demand instead of one-off spillover.
For expo logistics and shipping, the practical question is which venue and which week. Peak weeks compress dock windows and labor capacity; off-peak weeks open up the calendar.
The strongest programs match the venue to the audience. A sponsor expo at Central Bank Center reads differently than a board offsite at Griffin Gate or a brand dinner at The Campbell House.

sections.heading: Lexington convention venues and meeting properties
paragraphs: Central Bank Center at 430 W Vine carries the city's largest exhibit floor and runs on its own published rules and dimensions document.

The downtown host-hotel ring (Hyatt Regency Lexington at 401 W High, Hilton Lexington/Downtown at 369 W Vine, Lexington Marriott City Center at 121 W Vine) supports breakouts, sponsor rooms, and overflow.
Griffin Gate at 1800 Newtown Pike runs as Marriott's resort with meeting venues, a golf course, and a spa, which fits retreats and hospitality-heavy programs.
South Lexington adds The Campbell House at 1375 S Broadway with more than 25,000 square feet of meeting space and Embassy Suites Lexington Green at 245 Lexington Green Cir as the suite-hotel option.

sections.heading: Lexington labor and venue services
paragraphs: There is no single Lexington labor regime. Each venue runs its own published rules, which means the labor plan has to confirm against the current document for each show.
On any expo logistics and shipping program, the labor question gets answered against the venue's posted rules for that show. House versus outside crew splits change between buildings.
Utility and service orders file through each venue's portal. Discount cutoffs sit 30 to 45 days out; standard pricing kicks in after that; on-site rates apply during move-in.
Working principle: current manual, orders before cutoff, EAC packet on file with the GC if outside crew is involved.

sections.heading: Where Lexington shows cluster
paragraphs: Most Lexington shows cluster downtown around Central Bank Center, the host-hotel ring along Vine, High, and Main, and the walkable restaurant and hospitality blocks around Rupp Arena.

For expo logistics and shipping, the secondary clusters are the Newtown Pike resort corridor anchored on Griffin Gate, the south Lexington belt around The Campbell House and Embassy Suites Lexington Green, and the regional Bluegrass corridor (Versailles, Georgetown, Richmond, Frankfort, Nicholasville, Winchester) that extends the drive market.

sections.heading: Line items on a typical project
paragraphs: Most expo logistics and shipping budgets break down into the same line items in different proportions. Venue rates, footprint, and calendar shape which lines grow and which stay small.
Logistics scope covers outbound dispatch, advance warehouse versus direct routing, drayage submission, on-site receiving coordination, return freight, and the storage assignment for the next event.
Tight handoffs save shows. Each additional vendor is one more spot where a drawing detail or a labor call falls through.
Repeat programs add asset management to the line items. Storage, graphic swaps, refurb cycles, and re-crating get scheduled instead of improvised.
Personal shipments, exhibitor staff luggage, and lead-capture device shipping run on a separate path the client manages directly.

sections.heading: The process from start to finish
paragraphs: Expo logistics and shipping begins with five answers. Show, venue, audience, dates, budget. Those five frame the engagement.
Strategy and design phases yield the brief, the drawings, and a budget framed around real venue rates rather than estimates.
Coordination handles venue paperwork. Utility, rigging, labor, and EAC orders all file inside the published discount windows.
Production covers the build, graphics, and AV. The shop dry-fit is where install-day problems get found and fixed early.
Install operates inside the published move-in window with one lead on the floor. Show hours and strike continue under the same lead.
Debrief lands within 24 hours; the writeup informs the next show's planning.

sections.heading: Budget and timeline
paragraphs: Budget for expo logistics and shipping scales with how the booth is built, how big it is, what the venue charges for its services, what the graphics look like, how far the crates travel, and how many supervision days the program runs.

The line items on a typical expo logistics and shipping budget: structure, graphics, AV, show services, labor, freight, supervision. Round numbers without that breakdown tend to drift during execution.
Timeline: Outbound freight typically dispatches 5 to 10 days before show open for advance warehouse, or 1 to 2 days before for direct-to-show, depending on the venue's posted move-in window.

sections.heading: Outcomes we optimize for
paragraphs: The win condition for expo logistics and shipping is plain: install lands on schedule, the floor passes inspection, show hours run quiet, closeout sets up the next show.
Real measurement on expo logistics and shipping happens in leads, meetings, and pipeline. The booth and the install are the inputs that protect those metrics.
Debrief turns floor observations into design inputs for the next show. Demo, meeting, and graphic-zone data all carry forward.

paragraphs: For expo logistics and shipping, working with a Lexington-based shop matters most when something goes wrong on install day. A local team with an in-house shop solves most install-day problems in the time a national vendor needs to dispatch a flight.

Local presence also means the venue relationships are real. The same person calling the exhibitor services desk at Central Bank Center has been in the building enough times to know which approvals come back fast.

Across a multi-show calendar, the compounding effect of one team running expo logistics and shipping is fewer surprises and shorter install times. The fourth show runs on memory; the first one runs on first-principles planning.

custom-exhibit-booths.title: CUSTOM EXHIBIT BOOTHS
custom-exhibit-booths.heroLead: Custom exhibit booths in Lexington are designed and fabricated for one program, which means the structure, finishes, and brand language are dialed to the show, the venue, and the audience rather than reused from a rental kit.
intro: Custom booths are paid time at the design stage. The deliverable is a structure that feels like the brand's own building rather than a configured rental.
Lexington custom builds usually anchor a program at Central Bank Center or a downtown hotel ballroom, where the booth's finish quality and brand presence has room to land.
The trade-off is lead time and budget. Custom runs 16 to 20 weeks from brief to crate; rental runs 8 to 12. The cost gap reflects the design phase, the fabrication time, and the storage commitment.
These pages cover what a custom booth scope actually covers, how the design phase ties to fabrication, and when the custom path beats a rental on outcome.
focusList: Keep CUSTOM EXHIBIT BOOTHS tied to the actual show outcome, not a vague category label.
Treat the venue manual, freight timing, and access plan as the baseline for the build.
Align graphics, AV, and utility requests with the same calendar as the booth work.
Leave room for storage, reuse, and return freight so the next cycle starts cleanly.
Give the floor one clear lead so live decisions stay controlled during install and strike.

sections.heading: Lexington trade show market
paragraphs: The Lexington market for custom exhibit booths runs on a small set of venues and a clear regional drive radius. Central Bank Center anchors downtown; the host-hotel ring carries the breakouts; the Bluegrass corridor extends the audience.

The city's identity (university, healthcare, equine, bourbon, manufacturing) gives programs more than one buyer story to work with, which is why Lexington pulls real recurring demand instead of one-off spillover.
For custom exhibit booths, the practical question is which venue and which week. Peak weeks compress dock windows and labor capacity; off-peak weeks open up the calendar.
The strongest programs match the venue to the audience. A sponsor expo at Central Bank Center reads differently than a board offsite at Griffin Gate or a brand dinner at The Campbell House.

sections.heading: Where Lexington shows actually happen
paragraphs: The bulk of Lexington exhibit activity lives at Central Bank Center, with the city's largest floor and the connected ballroom and arena.
The downtown host-hotel ring around the convention campus handles breakouts, sponsor inventory, and the overflow that the convention floor pushes off-floor.
Outside downtown, Griffin Gate covers north Lexington's resort programming, while The Campbell House and Embassy Suites Lexington Green run the south-side suburban meeting belt.

sections.heading: The advantage of a local Lexington team
paragraphs: For custom exhibit booths, working with a Lexington-based shop matters most when something goes wrong on install day. A local team with an in-house shop solves most install-day problems in the time a national vendor needs to dispatch a flight.

Local presence also means the venue relationships are real. The same person calling the exhibitor services desk at Central Bank Center has been in the building enough times to know which approvals come back fast.

Across a multi-show calendar, the compounding effect of one team running custom exhibit booths is fewer surprises and shorter install times. The fourth show runs on memory; the first one runs on first-principles planning.

sections.heading: Who handles what during the Lexington install window
paragraphs: Inside the install window for custom exhibit booths, the named on-site lead holds the drawing, the punch list, and the labor calls. That role doesn't rotate; it stays with one person from crate-open through final strike.
The venue's exhibitor services desk handles utility orders, rigging approvals, and the published service paperwork. The show GC (where there is one) handles drayage and the master schedule.
The labor crew runs against the venue's labor model. At Central Bank Center the crew works the building's published rules; at downtown hotels the crew works through the property's events team.
Communication runs on a daily report during install, real-time updates during show hours, and a written debrief delivered within a day of strike.

sections.heading: Where Lexington shows cluster
paragraphs: Most Lexington shows cluster downtown around Central Bank Center, the host-hotel ring along Vine, High, and Main, and the walkable restaurant and hospitality blocks around Rupp Arena.

For custom exhibit booths, the secondary clusters are the Newtown Pike resort corridor anchored on Griffin Gate, the south Lexington belt around The Campbell House and Embassy Suites Lexington Green, and the regional Bluegrass corridor (Versailles, Georgetown, Richmond, Frankfort, Nicholasville, Winchester) that extends the drive market.

sections.heading: Line items on a typical project
paragraphs: Most custom exhibit booths budgets break down into the same line items in different proportions. Venue rates, footprint, and calendar shape which lines grow and which stay small.

Typical line items include booth structure (custom, modular, or rental), graphics and print, AV and lighting, show-services orders (power, internet, rigging) through the venue, labor calls against the venue's rules, freight in and out, on-site supervision, and post-show closeout.
Tight handoffs save shows. Each additional vendor is one more spot where a drawing detail or a labor call falls through.
Repeat programs add asset management to the line items. Storage, graphic swaps, refurb cycles, and re-crating get scheduled instead of improvised.
Out of scope by default are show organizer fees, booth lease costs paid to the show, client staff travel and per diems, and any data or lead-capture software the client runs internally.

sections.heading: From first call to strike
paragraphs: Step one on custom exhibit booths: the brief. Show, venue, dates, outcome all confirm before any other phase opens.
Strategy phase produces the brief; design phase produces the drawing set and the budget frame.
Phase three is venue paperwork: utilities, rigging, labor, EAC packets all file before the discount cutoffs close.
Production phase covers fabrication, graphics, AV, rentals, and the floor playbook. Dry-fit happens on the shop floor before crating.
Install honors the venue's published schedule. One named lead carries the install through show hours without rotation.
Closeout: strike, freight, debrief. The written debrief lands within a day and shapes the next show.

sections.heading: Budget and timeline
paragraphs: Budget for custom exhibit booths scales with how the booth is built, how big it is, what the venue charges for its services, what the graphics look like, how far the crates travel, and how many supervision days the program runs.
The line items on a typical custom exhibit booths budget: structure, graphics, AV, show services, labor, freight, supervision. Round numbers without that breakdown tend to drift during execution.
Timeline: Custom: 16 to 20 weeks. Rental: 8 to 12. Under 4 weeks is possible but narrows choices and raises cost.

sections.heading: What a successful project looks like
paragraphs: Successful custom exhibit booths executes against five tests: on-time install, first-walk inspection, quiet show hours, clean strike, documented closeout.
Real outcomes on custom exhibit booths sit in lead capture and pipeline data. The build and install protect those numbers; sloppy execution erodes them.
Within a week post-show, the booth's actual performance reads more clearly than any pre-show forecast. The debrief captures both technical and behavioral observations.

faqs.question: How much lead time is ideal?
faqs.answer: Custom builds run best with 16 to 20 weeks of lead time. Rentals take 8 to 12 weeks. Work inside 4 weeks is possible but raises costs and narrows the options.

faqs.question: What information kicks off a project?

faqs.question: Who runs the booth on-site?

Operationally, inlines work cleanly across all the Lexington venues. Central Bank Center, the downtown hotels, and the suburban properties can all support a 10x10 or 10x20 install on standard show-services orders.

island-exhibit-environments.title: ISLAND EXHIBIT ENVIRONMENTS
island-exhibit-environments.heroLead: Island exhibit environments in Lexington occupy four-sided floor positions with overhead rigging or hanging structure, integrated meeting and storage space, and architecture that has to read from every aisle.
intro: Island work is the most architecturally demanding booth type. No back wall, four sightlines, and overhead rigging that has to clear venue rules.
Lexington islands usually land at Central Bank Center where the floor heights and rigging path support hanging signs and overhead structures.

Operationally, island work needs rigging approvals filed against the venue's published rules, structural drawings for hanging elements, and an install plan that respects the show's posted move-in sequence.
These pages cover what island environment work includes, how rigging and hanging structure decisions get made, and where Central Bank Center's rules shape the build.

sections.heading: Lexington convention venues and meeting properties
paragraphs: Central Bank Center at 430 W Vine carries the city's largest exhibit floor and runs on its own published rules and dimensions document.

The downtown host-hotel ring (Hyatt Regency Lexington at 401 W High, Hilton Lexington/Downtown at 369 W Vine, Lexington Marriott City Center at 121 W Vine) supports breakouts, sponsor rooms, and overflow.
Griffin Gate at 1800 Newtown Pike runs as Marriott's resort with meeting venues, a golf course, and a spa, which fits retreats and hospitality-heavy programs.
South Lexington adds The Campbell House at 1375 S Broadway with more than 25,000 square feet of meeting space and Embassy Suites Lexington Green at 245 Lexington Green Cir as the suite-hotel option.

sections.heading: Lexington labor and venue services
paragraphs: Lexington's labor model is venue-specific, not citywide. Central Bank Center, the host hotels, and Griffin Gate each publish their own standards.
For island exhibit environments, the labor breakdown has to come from the manual. Each Lexington venue draws the line between in-house and outside work in its own way.
Each Lexington venue runs its own order portal for electrical, internet, rigging, and plumbing, with cutoff windows that usually close 30 to 45 days before doors.
Standard procedure: pull the manual, file the orders early, get the EAC packet to the GC well before badge day.

sections.heading: Lexington neighborhoods and event districts
paragraphs: Downtown holds the city's convention engine. Central Bank Center plus the connected hotels form the walkable event spine along Vine and High.

Beyond downtown, the map breaks into Newtown Pike's resort spine, the south Lexington suburban belt at Campbell House and Embassy Suites Lexington Green, and the Bluegrass drive corridor that ranges from Versailles through Winchester.

sections.heading: What's included in the project scope
paragraphs: Scope on a island exhibit environments engagement is downstream of the brief. The show, venue, audience, and date determine the line items.

Island environment scope covers four-sided architecture, integrated storage and meeting space, hanging sign or rigging structure, AV and lighting through the structure, graphics, and supervised install with rigging approvals filed against the venue's published rules.
Out of scope by default: show organizer fees, client travel and per diems, internal lead-capture and CRM tools, and vendor relationships the client manages directly.
On island exhibit environments programs that repeat, asset management folds into the scope. Storage, refurb cycles, graphic rotation, and freight loop planning all join the engagement.
The biggest savings come from scope clarity at kickoff. Install-day surprises almost always trace back to scope gaps, not labor mistakes.

sections.heading: The process from start to finish
paragraphs: The island exhibit environments arc opens with a brief that pins down the show context, the building, the audience, and the budget frame.
The strategy and design phases land the brief and the fabrication-ready drawing set; budget locks against real venue and freight numbers.
Phase three is the venue side. Orders go in before cutoff; EAC packets file with the GC; rigging approvals enter the queue.
Build phase covers fabrication, graphics, and AV. A shop dry-fit catches misalignments before they hit a venue dock.
The lead carries install through strike. Move-in, show hours, and move-out all run under one named owner.
Closeout includes a same-day debrief that turns floor observations into next-show inputs.

sections.heading: Cost and lead time
paragraphs: Cost for island exhibit environments scales with the venue's published rates, the booth's size, the freight pattern, and the supervision days the program needs.

The levers: build path (custom, modular, or rental), footprint, venue service fees, graphics, freight, and supervision. A small inline and a 30x50 island with a double deck don't share much beyond the same vocabulary.
Lead time for island exhibit environments: Custom: 16 to 20 weeks. Rental: 8 to 12. Anything inside 4 weeks is a rush, which narrows choices and raises cost.

sections.heading: What a successful project looks like
paragraphs: The shape of a good island exhibit environments project: install on schedule, venue walk-through passes, show hours run without callouts, strike honors the posted window.
The numbers that matter on island exhibit environments are leads, meetings, and pipeline. The booth and the install are inputs to those metrics.
Post-show, the floor data tells a better story than any pre-show model. The debrief documents what worked and what didn't.

paragraphs: Central Bank Center handles most of Lexington's serious exhibit work because no other venue in the city carries the same floor space and rigging support.
Steps from the convention center, the host-hotel ring (Hyatt, Hilton, Marriott City Center) absorbs breakout sessions, sponsor rooms, and overflow inventory.
Beyond the convention spine, Griffin Gate on Newtown Pike anchors the resort north side and the south-side suite hotels (Campbell House, Embassy Suites Lexington Green) hold suburban meetings.

sections.heading: Who handles what during the Lexington install window
paragraphs: Inside the install window for roadshow exhibit environments, the named on-site lead holds the drawing, the punch list, and the labor calls. That role doesn't rotate; it stays with one person from crate-open through final strike.
The venue's exhibitor services desk handles utility orders, rigging approvals, and the published service paperwork. The show GC (where there is one) handles drayage and the master schedule.
The labor crew runs against the venue's labor model. At Central Bank Center the crew works the building's published rules; at downtown hotels the crew works through the property's events team.
Communication runs on a daily report during install, real-time updates during show hours, and a written debrief delivered within a day of strike.

sections.heading: Where Lexington shows cluster
paragraphs: Most Lexington shows cluster downtown around Central Bank Center, the host-hotel ring along Vine, High, and Main, and the walkable restaurant and hospitality blocks around Rupp Arena.

For roadshow exhibit environments, the secondary clusters are the Newtown Pike resort corridor anchored on Griffin Gate, the south Lexington belt around The Campbell House and Embassy Suites Lexington Green, and the regional Bluegrass corridor (Versailles, Georgetown, Richmond, Frankfort, Nicholasville, Winchester) that extends the drive market.

sections.heading: Scope on a roadshow exhibit environments project
paragraphs: Scope on a roadshow exhibit environments project in Lexington starts from the show, the venue, and the date, then walks the line items the program actually needs rather than a generic exhibit menu.

Roadshow environment scope covers travel-rated structure, graphic packs that swap between cities, scheduled refurbishment, freight loop planning, repeatable install sequence, and crew scheduling that holds the calendar together across stops.

The boundary line matters: show organizer fees, client staff travel, and any software or vendors the client owns directly stay outside the roadshow exhibit environments engagement and route through coordination.
For programs that repeat, the roadshow exhibit environments scope adds storage, refurbishment, graphic rotation, and the freight loop that ties the next show on the calendar to this one.
A clean roadshow exhibit environments scope at the start saves the most money. Surprises during install are usually scope problems, not crew problems.

sections.heading: From first call to strike
paragraphs: Roadshow exhibit environments kicks off with a real conversation about the program's shape: show, venue, calendar, target outcome.
After the kickoff, strategy and design run together. Brief lands, floor plan drafts, budget shapes, drawings go to fabrication-ready.
Venue coordination follows design. The manual gets pulled, orders go in before cutoff, EAC paperwork files with the GC if outside crews are coming.
Build phase pulls all the trades together: shop floor, graphics, AV, rentals. Dry-fit before crating catches problems early.
Move-in works the venue's posted window with one lead carrying the floor; that same person stays through show hours.
Strike against the posted dismantle, freight handoff to the carrier, debrief within 24 hours. That document feeds the next planning cycle.

sections.heading: Cost and lead time
paragraphs: Cost for roadshow exhibit environments scales with the venue's published rates, the booth's size, the freight pattern, and the supervision days the program needs.

The levers: build path (custom, modular, or rental), footprint, venue service fees, graphics, freight, and supervision. A small inline and a 30x50 island with a double deck don't share much beyond the same vocabulary.
Lead time for roadshow exhibit environments: Custom: 16 to 20 weeks. Rental: 8 to 12. Anything inside 4 weeks is a rush, which narrows choices and raises cost.

sections.heading: Outcomes we optimize for
paragraphs: Success for roadshow exhibit environments reads as a list: timed install, no venue calls, smooth show hours, clean closeout that informs the next program.
The KPIs that matter for roadshow exhibit environments are leads captured, floor meetings booked, and pipeline created. Build quality and install quality flow into those numbers.
What the booth actually did during show hours becomes the input for the next round. Demo station traffic, meeting closes, dwell time at each graphic.

sections.heading: Why Trade Show Services of Lexington
paragraphs: The local model is simple: one shop, one team, one playbook for roadshow exhibit environments. Quality survives because handoffs are short.
Venue relationships compound with hours in the building. A local team has the rep needed to move a roadshow exhibit environments project through the convention center's published path quickly.
A roadshow exhibit environments calendar is cheaper per show as the team accumulates booth- and venue-specific knowledge. Less re-briefing, less re-training, less re-planning.

faqs.question: Do you work at Lexington Marriott City Center?
faqs.answer: Yes. the hotel at the heart of downtown Lexington. Projects at that building are planned against its specific floor rules.

faqs.question: How does labor work in Lexington?
faqs.answer: It runs venue by venue. Central Bank Center: the dimensions and specification sheet makes the convention campus a real exhibit and conference environment with current floor and access considerations that should shape the brief early. Other Lexington venues have their own published rules, and the plan matches the specific building.

faqs.question: How much lead time is ideal?
faqs.answer: Custom builds run best with 16 to 20 weeks of lead time. Rentals take 8 to 12 weeks. Work inside 4 weeks is possible but raises costs and narrows the options.

faqs.question: What information kicks off a project?
faqs.answer: The show, the venue, the booth number, the dates, and a target budget range. From those, an initial scope and a realistic cost come back within a few days.

faqs.question: Who runs the booth on-site?
faqs.answer: One named show-site lead runs the install, the show hours, and the strike. That person carries the floor plan and the punch list from crates to doors and reports up once a day.

faqs.question: Can the booth travel to the next city on our calendar?
faqs.answer: Yes — builds are designed for modular reuse unless single-use scenic is specifically required. Same structure, swappable graphics, standard crates. Lexington programs that roll to another city on the same calendar get handled end to end.

faqs.question: What happens if something breaks during show hours?
faqs.answer: The on-site lead handles it. Minor repairs happen in place; anything larger gets sourced from the local shop or rerouted through the venue's service desk.
